message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and independently determine whether this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in the CURRENT code (3 hardening passes already applied).

Finding G-010: numpy resample-stability path allocates a full R×N boolean matrix (peak-memory blowup) and lacks an at-scale equivalence/memory test
File: src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py:217
Description: The numpy branch of _resample_stability computes null_arr[None,:] >= res_arr[:,None] (and the <= variant) before .sum(axis=1), materializing a dense R×N boolean array — O(R·N) PEAK MEMORY, whereas the pure-Python fallback it claims to merely speed up is O(N). Confirmed at confirm.py:216-217. At corpus scale (R=10k resamples vs N=10k permutation null = 10^8 booleans ≈ 100 MB transient; 20k×20k ≈ 400 MB) enabling the optional numpy extra silently raises peak memory by orders of magnitude and can OOM where the pure path would not, while the docstring frames it as 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change'. Compounding this, the only equivalence test (test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python, null=40/res=25) is far below any size where the mask is observable and pins no memory contract, so a future searchsorted/chunked rewrite has no at-scale guardrail.
PoC: null=list(rng.normal(size=20000)); res=list(rng.normal(0.5,size=20000)); confirm._resample_stability(res, null, 'greater', 0.05) transiently allocates ~400MB (a 20000x20000 bool mask), whereas the pure path's working set is O(N). No existing test exercises this size or any memory characteristic.
Suggested fix: Sort the null once and use np.searchsorted for per-resample extreme counts in O((R+N)logN) time / O(R+N) memory: greater -> cnt = len(null) - searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'left'); less -> cnt = searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'right'); the (cnt+1)/(n+1)<=alpha test is byte-identical. If keeping the broadcast form, chunk the resamples and document the memory characteristic. Add a parametrized equivalence test at N=2000/R=2000 (and optionally a tracemalloc sub-quadratic peak-allocation regression test) so the rewrite is held to byte-identical results.

Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described in current code, the PoC is reproducible, and it is a genuine concern (not speculative, not already-handled, not intended design). Be concrete and cite file:line.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

160	    # so a hypothesis constructed by an unusual path (or a future field mutation) cannot confirm
161	    # against an unrecognized tail.
162	    if hypothesis.predicted_direction is not None and (
163	        hypothesis.predicted_direction not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS
164	    ):
165	        raise ValueError(
166	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got "
167	            f"{hypothesis.predicted_direction!r}"
168	        )
169	    _gate_preregistration(hypothesis, authority)
170	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B (rejects empty / discovery ids)
171	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
172	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
173	        statistic=statistic,
174	        p_value=p_value,
175	        alpha=alpha,
176	        powered=powered,
177	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
178	        test_description="held-out unit-level test",
179	    )
180	
181	
182	def _empirical_p(null: Sequence[float], observed: float, tail: Direction) -> float:
183	    """One-sided empirical p-value with +1 smoothing for the given tail."""
184	    n = len(null)
185	    if tail == "greater":
186	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v >= observed)
187	    else:
188	        extreme = sum(1 for v in null if v <= observed)
189	    return (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
190	
191	
192	def _resample_stability(
193	    resamples: Sequence[float], null: Sequence[float], tail: Direction, alpha: float
194	) -> float:
195	    """Fraction of resamples that **reproduce the observed effect**.
196	
197	    Each resample is scored as its own observed statistic against the null and must itself be
198	    significant in the predicted tail (empirical p ≤ ``alpha``). This measures reproduction of
199	    the observed *effect* — not merely landing on the predicted side of the null center, which a
200	    resample distribution clearing the null median by an epsilon would satisfy while reproducing
201	    nothing of the observed magnitude (audit pass 2, #139). The closer to 1.0, the more
202	    consistently the effect's significance reproduces across resamples.
203	
204	    The default path is O(R·N) pure Python (one null pass per resample). When numpy is available
205	    (the optional ``baseline`` extra) the whole R×N comparison runs as a single vectorized op
206	    (pass 3, #F-017). The numpy path computes the IDENTICAL integer extreme-counts and the same
207	    ``(extreme + 1) / (n + 1) <= alpha`` test, so its result is exactly equal to the Python path;
208	    it is purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change.
209	    """
210	    n = len(null)
211	    try:
212	        import numpy as np
213	
214	        null_arr = np.asarray(null, dtype=np.float64)
215	        res_arr = np.asarray(resamples, dtype=np.float64)
216	        if tail == "greater":
217	            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
218	        else:
219	            extreme = (null_arr[None, :] <= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)
220	        p = (extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
221	        return float(int((p <= alpha).sum()) / len(resamples))
222	    except ImportError:
223	        significant = sum(1 for r in resamples if _empirical_p(null, r, tail) <= alpha)
224	        return significant / len(resamples)
225	
226	
227	_VALID_DIRECTIONS = ("greater", "less")
228	
229	
230	def _resolve_direction(hypothesis: Hypothesis, predicted_direction: Direction | None) -> Direction:
231	    """Direction is fixed at lock time on the hypothesis (issue #24); the confirm-time
232	    argument is optional and, if given, must match the pre-registered one.
233	
234	    The resolved direction is validated against ``{"greater", "less"}`` and any other value is
235	    rejected: an unrecognized direction would otherwise fall through to the 'less' tail in
236	    :func:`_empirical_p`, silently flipping supported↔contradicted (audit pass 2, #128).
237	    """
238	    locked_dir = hypothesis.predicted_direction
239	    if locked_dir is not None:
240	        if predicted_direction is not None and predicted_direction != locked_dir:
241	            raise ValueError(
242	                "predicted_direction argument contradicts the hypothesis's pre-registered "
243	                f"direction ({predicted_direction!r} != {locked_dir!r})"
244	            )
245	        resolved = locked_dir
246	    elif predicted_direction is None:
247	        raise ValueError(
248	            "no predicted_direction: set it on the hypothesis at pre-registration "
249	            "(preferred) or pass it explicitly"
250	        )
251	    else:
252	        resolved = predicted_direction
253	    if resolved not in _VALID_DIRECTIONS:
254	        raise ValueError(
255	            f"predicted_direction must be one of {_VALID_DIRECTIONS}; got {resolved!r} — an "
256	            "unrecognized tail would silently flip the supported/contradicted verdict"
257	        )
258	    return resolved
259	
260	
261	def confirm_whole_corpus(
262	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
263	    *,
264	    observed: float,
265	    null_distribution: Sequence[float],
266	    alpha: float,
267	    resample_statistics: Sequence[float] | None = None,
268	    stability_threshold: float = 0.9,
269	    predicted_direction: Direction | None = None,

content.text:
The finding is accurate on all counts. The test uses null=40/res=25 (the finding said null=40/res=25, matching), far below any scale where the O(R·N) memory matters, and pins no memory contract.

Let me verify the suggested searchsorted equivalence is correct, since that's load-bearing to whether the fix is byte-identical.

For `tail == "greater"`: `extreme = count of null values >= res`. With sorted null, `searchsorted(null_sorted, res, 'left')` gives the count of values `< res`, so `len - searchsorted(..., 'left')` = count of values `>= res`. Correct.

For `tail == "less"`: `extreme = count of null <= res` = `searchsorted(null_sorted, res, 'right')`. Correct.

The PoC: with numpy installed, `_resample_stability` with R=N=20000 allocates a 20000×20000 bool array = 4×10^8 bytes ≈ 400MB transient. The numpy import is wrapped in try/except so it only triggers when the `baseline` extra is installed. numpy is not installed in this environment, so the PoC isn't directly executable here, but the code path is unambiguous and the broadcast `null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]` provably materializes a full R×N array.

Let me confirm there's no chunking or guard elsewhere that limits R or N.

content.text:
Confirmed. `resample_statistics` and `null_distribution` are caller-supplied sequences with no size cap or chunking. The `baseline` extra installs numpy (1.26+), so in any deployment with that extra, the broadcast path is active. The docstring (lines 204-208) explicitly frames numpy as "purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change" — yet for large R·N it is precisely where the O(R·N) peak-memory blowup bites, which is the asymmetry the finding describes.

Assessment:
- Code matches exactly (confirm.py:216-219).
- Broadcast provably materializes a dense R×N bool array before reduction. The pure-Python fallback streams in O(N) working set per resample.
- No size guard or chunking anywhere; inputs are arbitrary caller-supplied sequences.
- The only equivalence test is at null=40/res=25, pins no memory contract.
- The suggested `searchsorted` fix is byte-identical (verified the `left`/`right` semantics above).

The PoC isn't directly runnable here (numpy not in this sandbox), but the code path is unambiguous and the memory math is straightforward. This is a real, non-speculative production concern, though severity is moderate: it requires the optional `baseline` extra AND corpus-scale R·N to manifest, and 400MB transient is a degradation/OOM-risk rather than a correctness or security defect. The numbers in the description (10^8 booleans ≈ 100MB, 20k×20k = 4×10^8 ≈ 400MB) are arithmetically correct (numpy bool dtype = 1 byte).

input.reasoning:
Verified against current code. confirm.py:216-219 computes `(null_arr[None, :] >= res_arr[:, None]).sum(axis=1)` (and the `<=` variant for the 'less' tail), which materializes a dense R×N boolean array BEFORE the axis-1 reduction. numpy bool dtype is 1 byte/element, so R=N=20000 transiently allocates 4×10^8 bytes ≈ 400MB (10k×10k ≈ 100MB) — exactly as the finding states. The pure-Python fallback (confirm.py:223) streams one null pass per resample with an O(N) working set, so the numpy path is asymptotically worse in PEAK MEMORY, not merely faster. The docstring (lines 204-208) explicitly claims the numpy path is "purely a speed optimization for large R·N, never a semantic change," which is the precise asymmetry the finding flags: large R·N is where the blowup occurs.

Inputs are unbounded: resample_statistics and null_distribution are caller-supplied sequences with no size cap or chunking anywhere (confirm.py:310-315). The `baseline` optional extra installs numpy>=1.26 (pyproject.toml:68-69) and the prod extras bundle it (line 73), so this path is live in real deployments. The numpy import is try/except-guarded, so it only activates when the extra is present.

The only equivalence test (tests/test_confirm.py:191-208) runs at null=40/res=25 — far below any scale where the dense mask is observable — and pins no memory/peak-allocation contract, so a future searchsorted/chunked rewrite has no at-scale or memory guardrail. The suggested searchsorted fix is byte-identical: for 'greater', count(null >= res) = len(null) - searchsorted(null_sorted, res, 'left'); for 'less', count(null <= res) = searchsorted(null_sorted, res, 'right'); both feed the same (cnt+1)/(n+1) <= alpha test.

PoC could not be executed directly because numpy is not installed in this verification sandbox (python3 -c 'import numpy' -> ModuleNotFoundError), but the broadcast semantics and 1-byte bool dtype make the allocation deterministic and the memory arithmetic correct; the code path is unambiguous.

Severity is medium, not high: it requires BOTH the optional baseline extra AND corpus-scale R·N to manifest, the impact is a transient memory degradation / OOM risk rather than any correctness, semantic, or security defect (results remain identical), and the workaround (omit the extra) restores the O(N) path. It is a genuine production-readiness/scalability concern with a missing test contract, not speculative or intended design.

content.text: Verification complete. Finding G-010 is confirmed as a true production-readiness concern at medium severity. Details returned via StructuredOutput.
